message.content:
Repo: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1, fixes applied vs main). Venv: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
The file /tmp/pass1_confirm.json is a JSON array of 22 pass-1 findings (each: issue, title, file, sev, problem, suggested_fix).
Constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign, no metered API, engine imports no adapter, lazy optional backends.

You are independent CONFIRMER #2 of 2 for finding INDEX 12 (0-based) in /tmp/pass1_confirm.json.
Read that item (its issue number, problem, suggested_fix, file). Then VERIFY THE APPLIED FIX on the current branch: read the changed source AND its regression test (git diff vs main, or just read the file + tests), and where useful run a PoC against the venv to confirm the original problem is actually resolved.
Return: appropriate = the fix correctly addresses the root cause in a sound way (not a hack/incomplete patch/over-claim); complete = the fix fully resolves the finding AND has a regression test that would catch a regression. Set either false if not, and say exactly what is missing. Echo the issue number in 'issue'.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the finding at index 12.

content.content:
1	[{"issue": "101", "title": "Credential scrubber lets the high-stakes subprocess be redirected off-box (data exfiltration) and switched to a metered provider", "file": "src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py", "sev": "HIGH", "problem": "scrubbed_env() (the env passed to the claude_agent_sdk subprocess at line 336) only removes vars whose NAME contains 'KEY' or 'TOKEN' under the ANTHROPIC_ prefix (is_metered_anthropic_credential, lines 113-120). It is a deny-by-name filter, not an allowlist, so every other environment variable is forwarded verbatim to the Claude CLI/Agent SDK subprocess. Multiple Claude Code env vars that contain neither 'KEY' nor 'TOKEN' subvert the two hard invariants this module exists to enforce:

1) DATA SOVEREIGNTY (ADR-0003) bypass: ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL (and ANTHROPIC_BEDROCK_BASE_URL / ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_B", "suggested_fix": "Make the subprocess env a strict allowlist instead of a name-based denylist, OR extend the denylist to strip the provider-redirecting / metered-provider switches. Concretely: also drop ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_BEDROCK_BASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_BASE_URL, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX, CLAUDE_CODE_API_KEY_HELPER, HTTP_PROXY, HTTPS_PROXY, ALL_PROXY (and their lowercase forms),"}, {"issue": "102", "title": "run_json applies retries multiplicatively, causing a retry-storm and unbounded blocking time with no overall deadline", "file": "src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py", "sev": "HIGH", "problem": "`run_json` loops `MAX_RETRIES+1` times (line 531) and each iteration calls `_run_with_retries` (line 542), which itself retries up to `MAX_RETRIES` transient attempts AND up to `RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES` rate-limit attempts. The budgets multiply. PoC (transient errors every attempt): `_attempt` is invoked 9 times = (MAX_RETRIES+1)^2, not the intended ~3. Worse, the rate-limit path: per outer iteration the rate budget sleeps sum(RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF*(r+1) for r in 0..4) = 450s; times 3 outer iterations = 1350s of pure backoff sleep, PLUS up to (HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT+30)=330s of actual work per attemp", "suggested_fix": "Do not nest two retry layers. Either (a) call `_attempt`+`extract_json` directly inside run_json's loop (no inner `_run_with_retries`), or (b) keep `_run_with_retries` and have run_json only re-roll on JSON *parse* failures (not on transient errors the inner loop already handled). Add an absolute wall-clock deadline budget shared across all retries/backoffs so a single call cannot block indefinite"}, {"issue": "103", "title": "CI integration lane runs `pytest --cov` but pytest-cov is not a declared dependency — the lane fails on every run", "file": ".github/workflows/ci.yml", "sev": "HIGH", "problem": "The integration job's final step runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing`. The `--cov` flag is provided by the `pytest-cov` plugin, but pytest-cov appears nowhere in pyproject.toml — not in the `dev` extra (which only pins pytest, ruff, mypy) and not pulled transitively by any heavy extra. The lane installs `.[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]`, none of which provide pytest-cov or coverage. I verified in a clean Python 3.14 venv: (1) `pip install 'pytest>=8'` does not bring pytest_cov (`importlib.util.find_spec('pytest_cov')` is None); (2) run", "suggested_fix": "Add `pytest-cov>=5` (and, if used directly, `coverage`) to the `dev` optional-dependency group in pyproject.toml so the plugin is installed in both CI lanes. Alternatively, gate `--cov` behind a separate `dev` tool group and confirm it is installed before the integration step. After fixing, the integration lane should be re-run to confirm it passes end-to-end."}, {"issue": "104", "title": "Pool-saturation guard raises PermanentReasoningError for a transient condition, so concurrent callers fail with no retry or backpressure", "file": "src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "When all 8 worker slots are in-flight (e.g. 8 concurrent slow/hung backend calls), `_submit_bounded` raises `PermanentReasoningError` (line 153). But `_run_with_retries` catches `PermanentReasoningError` FIRST and re-raises immediately with no retry and no backoff (seam.py:448-449). Saturation is a *temporary* contention/backpressure condition, not a permanent fault, yet it is classified as fatal. PoC: filling all 8 slots with hung calls that time out at 0.2s leaves `_inflight==8`; a subsequent trivial fast call is refused with `PermanentReasoningError("reasoning timeout pool saturated")`. Aft", "suggested_fix": "Raise a retryable signal on saturation instead of `PermanentReasoningError` — e.g. a `RateLimitError`/dedicated backpressure error that `_run_with_retries` retries with bounded backoff — or block with a bounded wait (semaphore acquire with timeout) so callers queue briefly rather than fail. Reserve `PermanentReasoningError` for genuinely unrecoverable saturation (e.g. confirmed-hung slots that nev"}, {"issue": "105", "title": "Postgres ConnectionPool is leaked (never closed) when schema bootstrap fails after the pool opens", "file": "src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "In `get_checkpointer`, the pool is opened and registered (line 182-190), then schema bootstrap runs inside the try (lines 194-210). If Postgres is reachable but bootstrap fails (missing CREATE privileges, advisory-lock error, a `setup()` DDL error, transient connection drop during DDL), the `except Exception` at line 212 builds a redacted message and re-raises `RuntimeError` — but the already-open pool is NEVER closed. It remains in `_OPEN_POOLS` (so it is only freed at process exit) holding min_size=1 live connection plus a background worker thread. PoC: stubbing `ConnectionPool` so `.connect", "suggested_fix": "In the `except` handler (or a `finally`/try-around-bootstrap), close the pool that was opened in this call before re-raising — e.g. `_safe_close(pool)` and remove it from `_OPEN_POOLS` — so a failed init does not leak the connection/worker thread it just created."}, {"issue": "106", "title": "cosine_similarity_matrix converts the O(n^2) numpy result to a nested Python list, ~70x-ing peak memory and serializing the win", "file": "src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "_cosine_matrix_numpy computes the n x n similarity matrix vectorized (good), then at line 112 returns `[[float(v) for v in row] for row in m]` — a full nested Python list of n^2 native float objects. A numpy float64 cell is 8 bytes; a boxed Python float is ~24 bytes plus list-pointer overhead (~8 B) and the per-row list headers, i.e. roughly 32-56 B/cell, ~4-7x the dense array. The conversion is also a pure-Python O(n^2) loop that dominates runtime at scale. PoC against the venv (numpy 2.4.6): n=3000, d=384 (MiniLM-size corpus) took 7.76 s and 390 MB of tracked Python allocation; the returned ", "suggested_fix": "Return the numpy ndarray (or a thin read-only view) from the numpy path instead of boxing to nested lists, letting callers index without the 4-7x blow-up; or expose a streaming/blocked API (e.g. top-k per row, or a generator over row blocks) so the full n x n matrix is never materialized. If a list-of-lists must stay for the pure path's contract, gate the dense materialization behind an explicit `"}, {"issue": "107", "title": "BaselineArtifact deep-freezes its entire contents element-by-element, turning a similarity matrix into a recursively tuple-ized copy", "file": "src/assay_engine/_frozen.py", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "BaselineArtifact.__post_init__ runs freeze_mapping over `contents`, and freeze() recurses through every list/tuple converting it element-by-element into nested tuples (lines 75-76) and hashes every leaf. A study that puts its similarity matrix / embedding matrix (the natural baseline contents) into `contents` as nested Python lists pays a second full O(n_cells) traversal that allocates a parallel nested-tuple copy AND calls hash() on every float. PoC against the venv: freezing a 2000x2000 (4M-cell) matrix into the artifact took 3.29 s and 64 MB on top of the original. Combined with the previou", "suggested_fix": "Do not deep-freeze large numeric payloads cell-by-cell. The freeze() already has array-like handling (lines 85-94) that canonicalizes objects exposing tobytes()/shape to a single (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor in O(1) wrappers — steer builders/contracts toward numpy/array.array for bulk numeric content so this fast path is taken, and/or add a shallow-immutable container for large arrays so conten"}, {"issue": "108", "title": "No supply-chain vulnerability scanning (pip-audit) in CI for a security/privacy-focused production engine", "file": ".github/workflows/ci.yml", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "Neither pyproject.toml nor ci.yml contains any dependency-vulnerability scanning step (`grep -n 'pip-audit\|safety\|bandit'` returns nothing). The project pulls a large, fast-moving transitive surface through its extras — verified by resolving the `observability` extra alone, which installs 150+ packages including cryptography, aiohttp, Flask/Werkzeug, requests, protobuf, grpcio, openai, anthropic, boto3, and SQLAlchemy. With only floor+major-cap ranges (e.g. `cryptography` unconstrained as a transitive, `aiohttp` transitive, `requests` transitive), a clean install on any future day can pull a", "suggested_fix": "Add a CI job (or step in the integration lane) that runs `pip-audit` against the installed environment, failing on known vulnerabilities (with an allowlist file for accepted/unfixable advisories). Consider also committing a constraints/lock file (e.g. `pip-compile` output or `uv.lock`) for the heavy extras so installs are reproducible and audited, while keeping pyproject ranges permissive for libr"}, {"issue": "109", "title": "A raised run leaves a partial provenance trail with no failure entry — the abort reason is unrecoverable from the trail", "file": "/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "GOVERNANCE.md §3 and the docstring at lines 102-103 explicitly promise that "a caller may pass its own trail to run_study so a run that *raises* (a blocked gate, a firewall violation) still leaves an auditable partial trail." That partial trail is auditable for what *succeeded*, but it records NOTHING about the failure itself: when any phase raises (IngestionError, FirewallViolation, PreRegistrationError, GateError, or an adapter exception), control leaves through the bare try/finally at lines 269/522 and the finally block only calls tracker.end_run — it never records a provenance entry for th", "suggested_fix": "Wrap the phase machine body in try/except/finally: on any exception, before re-raising, record a terminal entry e.g. trail.record('run_failed', f'{type(exc).__name__} during {visited[-1].name if visited else "start"}', error_type=type(exc).__name__, message=str(exc), last_phase=visited[-1].name if visited else None). Keep it inside the chain (so verify_records still passes) and guard the record() "}, {"issue": "110", "title": "Failed runs are terminated in the experiment tracker as FINISHED — a failed run is indistinguishable from a successful one in the metric store", "file": "/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "run_study's finally block calls tracker.end_run(run_id) on every exit path, including when a phase raised. MlflowExperimentTracker.end_run calls client.set_terminated(run_id) with no status argument, and MLflow's set_terminated defaults status to "FINISHED" (confirmed from the installed mlflow: the signature is set_terminated(run_id, status=None, ...) and the docstring states it "Defaults to FINISHED"). Result: a run that aborted on a firewall violation or a blocked gate is recorded in the on-box experiment store with status FINISHED, identical to a clean completion. Operators querying the tra", "suggested_fix": "Distinguish success from failure: capture the outcome in run_study (e.g. set an `ok` flag true only after trail.verify() succeeds) and pass a status into end_run. Add a status parameter to ExperimentTracker.end_run / MlflowExperimentTracker.end_run (default 'FINISHED', pass 'FAILED' on the exception path) so set_terminated(run_id, status=status) marks aborted runs FAILED. The set_terminated call a"}, {"issue": "111", "title": "Graph-orchestration gate decisions never enter the hash-chained provenance trail — the documented “append-only trail” for the durable path is unchained, untamper-evident LangGraph state", "file": "/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "There are two governance mechanisms (ADR-0010): the synchronous run_study path records every gate decision into the hash-chained ProvenanceTrail (provenance.py), and the durable LangGraph interrupt/resume path (gatenode.make_gate_node) records decisions as plain dicts appended to a LangGraph state list 'gate_decisions'. GOVERNANCE §3 states the chained ProvenanceTrail "is the spine… No step can be retroactively removed or reordered," and GOVERNANCE.md lines 47-50 call the graph's gate_decisions list "the append-only trail." But the graph path's decisions are NEVER written into the chained Prov", "suggested_fix": "Bridge graph gate decisions into the chained trail: have make_gate_node (or a thin wrapper) accept a ProvenanceTrail-backed recorder and record each validated decision via trail.record('gate', ...) at apply time, so the durable path's approvals/rejections land in the same hash-chained, verify_records-checkable trail as the synchronous path. At minimum, reconcile the docs to stop calling the unchai"}, {"issue": "112", "title": "LocalDataVersioner.path_for performs no version-id validation, enabling path traversal outside the store", "file": "src/assay_engine/persistence/versioning.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "path_for(digest) builds self._root / digest[:2] / digest with no validation that digest is a hex SHA-256. A caller passing a digest sourced from untrusted data (e.g. a version id round-tripped through provenance records or a model/JSON reply) can escape the store: path_for('../../etc/passwd') -> /tmp/store/../../../etc/passwd, path_for('/etc/passwd') -> /etc/passwd (verified against the venv). LocalDataVersioner and this method are part of the exported public persistence API (persistence/__init__.py), so an adapter that retrieves an artifact by an externally-influenced id can read/return files", "suggested_fix": "Validate the digest before composing the path: reject anything not matching ^[0-9a-f]{64}$ (raise ValueError), or after resolving assert the result is within self._root.resolve() (Path.is_relative_to). Apply the same check in any future getter that maps an id to a filesystem path."}, {"issue": "113", "title": "GateReview is a frozen dataclass whose Mapping payload is not frozen — mutable-in-place and unhashable (issue #7 escape)", "file": "src/assay_engine/pipeline.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "GateReview is declared @dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True) with a `payload: Mapping[str, Any]` field but, unlike EVERY other frozen mapping-bearing value record in the engine (ProvenanceEntry, GateDecision, Corpus, ClaimRecord, Verdict, Hypothesis, BaselineArtifact, ReasoningRequest, etc.), it has no __post_init__ that normalizes the field through freeze_mapping. This reproduces both halves of the codebase's own documented issue #7 (see src/assay_engine/_frozen.py module docstring):

1. Mutable-in-place: `frozen=True` only blocks attribute rebinding; the contained dict stays live, so a 'frozen", "suggested_fix": "Add the same __post_init__ used by sibling records, e.g. in pipeline.py:

    from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping  # add to imports

    @dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
    class GateReview:
        ...
        payload: Mapping[str, Any]
        def __post_init__(self) -> None:
            object.__setattr__(self, 'payload', freeze_mapping(self.payload))

This makes payload deep-im"}, {"issue": "114", "title": "ProvenanceTrail.record performs a non-atomic check-then-act on shared list state (not thread-safe)", "file": "src/assay_engine/provenance.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "record() computes `seq = len(self._entries)` and `prev_hash = self._entries[-1].entry_hash` (read), then does work (clock, digest, freeze), then `self._entries.append(entry)` (write), with no lock. If a single ProvenanceTrail were shared across threads, two concurrent record() calls could read the same seq/prev_hash and append two entries that both claim e.g. seq=N with the same prev_hash, breaking the chain so verify_records() (which asserts e.seq == i and e.prev_hash == prev) would fail — a corrupted append-only audit trail, the exact 'no step can be removed or reordered' guarantee the trail", "suggested_fix": "Guard the read-compute-append sequence with a threading.Lock held across the seq/prev_hash read through the append (or document the trail as single-thread-only and assert it). Given record() also calls an injected clock and a hashing step, hold the lock for the whole method to keep seq/prev_hash/entry_hash mutually consistent."}, {"issue": "115", "title": "Measurement↔Interpretation fence misses dict keys (and non-container objects): an Interpretation can be smuggled into a Measurement", "file": "/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/fence.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "METHODOLOGY.md §6 and the fence.py docstring state the fence "refuses at construction to hold an Interpretation (even nested)" and the Measurement.__post_init__ comment claims it is blocked "at the top level OR nested in a container/metadata (issues #22, #30)." The recursive guard _contains_interpretation does NOT honor that for two cases, both reproducible against the venv:

1) Mapping KEYS. _contains_interpretation recurses only into Mapping VALUES (`any(_contains_interpretation(v) for v in obj.values())`, line 44). An Interpretation is frozen+hashable, so it is a legal dict key. PoC: `Measu", "suggested_fix": "In _contains_interpretation, for the Mapping branch also scan keys: `any(_contains_interpretation(k) or _contains_interpretation(v) for k, v in obj.items())`. That closes case (1) fully and is a small, contained change. For case (2), either (a) tighten the docstrings/METHODOLOGY §6 to state explicitly that the structural guard covers only JSON-native containers and that wrapping judgment in a cust"}, {"issue": "116", "title": "Raw RuntimeError from a shut-down reasoning pool escapes the public ReasoningSeam API un-normalized", "file": "src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "`_submit_bounded` calls `_pool.submit(fn)`; if the shared module-global pool has been shut down (which happens during interpreter shutdown, or if any cleanup ever calls `.shutdown()`), `ThreadPoolExecutor.submit` raises `RuntimeError('cannot schedule new futures after shutdown')`. The `except BaseException` at line 160 only decrements `_inflight` and re-raises the raw `RuntimeError` (line 163). It is never normalized to a `ReasoningError`. PoC: after `seam._pool.shutdown(wait=True)`, a call to `_with_timeout(...)` raises a bare `RuntimeError` that escapes `TieredReasoningSeam.run()`. A caller ", "suggested_fix": "Catch the submit-time `RuntimeError` (or convert `BaseException`/`RuntimeError` from submit) into a `PermanentReasoningError`/`ReasoningError` with a clear message (e.g. 'reasoning pool is shut down') before re-raising, so the public seam only ever surfaces the documented error hierarchy."}, {"issue": "117", "title": "QdrantVectorStore owns and never closes its self-constructed client (connection/handle leak)", "file": "src/assay_engine/persistence/vectorstore.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "When no client is injected, `QdrantVectorStore.__init__` constructs its own `QdrantClient` via `get_qdrant_client()` (line 64) and stores it on `self._client`, but the class exposes no `close()`/context-manager and never closes the client. `QdrantClient` holds an underlying HTTP connection pool (httpx) / gRPC channel; for a self-constructed client the store is the sole owner, so the connection resources are leaked for the lifetime of every store instance with no deterministic release. Long-running processes that create many short-lived stores accumulate client connection pools.", "suggested_fix": "Make `QdrantVectorStore` a context manager (`__enter__`/`__exit__`) and/or add a `close()` that calls `self._client.close()` — but only for clients it created itself (track an `_owns_client` flag so an injected client, owned by the caller, is not closed)."}, {"issue": "118", "title": "QdrantVectorStore.upsert materializes all PointStructs (with per-vector copies) before sending any batch, so batching bounds payload but not memory", "file": "src/assay_engine/persistence/vectorstore.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "upsert builds the complete `points` list — one PointStruct per id, each holding `list(v)` (a fresh copy of the whole vector) — for the entire input before the chunking loop at line 88 sends anything. The comment claims chunking handles 'a large ingest', but it only bounds each HTTP request payload; peak process memory still holds the full corpus of vectors duplicated into PointStructs at once. For a real ingest (e.g. 5M docs x 768 float dims) this is the entire embedding set re-copied into Python objects in RAM simultaneously, which on a single Mac mini node is the OOM path the batch loop appe", "suggested_fix": "Iterate over `zip(ids, vectors)` and build PointStructs one batch at a time inside the loop (or accept an Iterable and pull batch_size items per request), so only one batch of points exists in memory at any moment. Validate len equality lazily or require equal-length up front without forcing full materialization. This makes the batch loop bound memory as well as request size."}, {"issue": "119", "title": "corpus_fingerprint is recomputed redundantly per study (ingest + every build_baseline_artifact call), each a full canonical copy + sort + JSON of the whole corpus", "file": "src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "corpus_fingerprint builds a full list-comprehension copy of every unit and relation, sorts all units by id, canonicalizes every attribute mapping via _plain, and json.dumps the whole thing — O(total corpus bytes) time and memory. run_study computes it once at ingest (pipeline.py:280, `cfp`). Then build_baseline_artifact (determinism.py:104) computes it again from scratch for the determinism record's `input_hashes['corpus']` and `corpus_fingerprint`; a study's BaselineBuilder that calls build_baseline_artifact thus repeats the full-corpus canonicalization a second (and the builder may itself fi", "suggested_fix": "Compute corpus_fingerprint once and pass it into build_baseline_artifact (e.g. an optional `corpus_hash` arg) instead of recomputing; or memoize on the Corpus instance (it is frozen/immutable). Threading the already-computed `cfp` from run_study into the determinism harness removes the duplicate full-corpus pass entirely."}, {"issue": "120", "title": "Package version is hardcoded in two independent places (pyproject `version` and `__init__.__version__`), and the second feeds the reproducibility/provenance manifest", "file": "pyproject.toml", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "`pyproject.toml` sets `version = "0.0.1"` (line 7) while `src/assay_engine/__init__.py` separately hardcodes `__version__ = "0.0.1"` (line 28). These are two independent strings with no single source of truth — hatchling is not configured for dynamic versioning (`grep dynamic`/`hatch.version` returns nothing). This is not merely a style nit: `src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py` imports `__version__ as _ENGINE_VERSION` (line 24) and stamps it into the determinism/provenance record as `component_versions["engine"]` (line 111), which is part of the hash-chained reproducibility trail. A rele", "suggested_fix": "Single-source the version. Either: (a) set `dynamic = ["version"]` in pyproject and add `[tool.hatch.version] path = "src/assay_engine/__init__.py"` so hatchling reads `__version__` from the module; or (b) drop the literal from `__init__.py` and derive it at runtime via `importlib.metadata.version("assay-engine")`. Option (a) keeps the determinism manifest source authoritative and removes the drif"}, {"issue": "121", "title": "mypy is pinned to `python_version = "3.11"` but CI advertises and matrix-tests up to 3.14, so 3.12–3.14-only type errors are never caught", "file": "pyproject.toml", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "`[tool.mypy] python_version = "3.11"` forces mypy to type-check the code as if running on Python 3.11 regardless of the runner's interpreter. The core CI lane runs mypy across a 3.11–3.14 matrix and the integration lane runs it on 3.12, but because the analysis target is fixed at 3.11, type behavior that differs across versions (e.g. stdlib stubs that gained/changed signatures in 3.12+, version-gated typing constructs) is only ever validated at the 3.11 floor. The project classifies and matrix-tests 3.14 as supported, so the type checker should also exercise the newer targets to make that supp", "suggested_fix": "Either remove the fixed `python_version` so mypy infers the target from the running interpreter (letting the matrix exercise each version), or run an explicit mypy invocation per matrix Python (`mypy --python-version ${{ matrix.python-version }}`). Keep the lazy-backend overrides as-is."}, {"issue": "122", "title": "run_study emits phase/reasoning spans but never starts tracing or stamps run_id baggage — traces cannot be correlated to the run or the experiment-tracker run_id", "file": "/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "run_study is the documented end-to-end composed runner and it opens trace spans for every phase (tracer.span('phase:INGEST') etc.) and the reasoning seam emits LLM/AGENT spans underneath. But run_study never calls bootstrap_tracing() and never wraps execution in run_trace_context(run_id) — grep of pipeline.py shows no reference to either. run_trace_context (tracing.py:145-163) is what stamps the 'assay.run_id' baggage onto every span so traces are correlatable across stores; the orchestration graph runner (graph.py:104-108) does exactly this, but the primary pipeline does not. Consequence: spa", "suggested_fix": "In run_study, after obtaining run_id (or a synthesized correlation id when no tracker is supplied), call bootstrap_tracing() once and wrap the phase machine in `with run_trace_context(corr_id):` so every phase and reasoning span is stamped with the run id and lands at the local collector — mirroring orchestration.graph.run(). Use the experiment_run_id when present so Phoenix spans and the MLflow r"}]

content.content:
tests/test_immutability.py
===diff tests===
 tests/test_baseline.py            | 27 +++++++++++
 tests/test_contract.py            | 10 ++++
 tests/test_orchestration_graph.py | 17 +++++++
 tests/test_persistence.py         | 64 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 tests/test_pipeline.py            | 42 +++++++++++++++--
 tests/test_reasoning.py           | 97 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++------
 6 files changed, 240 insertions(+), 17 deletions(-)

1	"""Deep-immutable, hashable mapping for the engine's frozen value records.
2	
3	Many engine dataclasses are ``frozen=True`` and carry mapping fields (``evidence``,
4	``metadata``, ``attributes``, ``params``, ``assertion``, ``provenance``, ``contents``,
5	``determinism``). With a plain ``dict`` default two problems arise (audit pass 1, issue #7):
6	
7	1. ``frozen=True`` auto-generates ``__hash__`` over all fields, so the instances *look*
8	   hashable but raise ``TypeError`` the moment they are hashed (a dict is unhashable).
9	2. ``frozen`` only blocks attribute rebinding; the contained dict stays mutable in place,
10	   so a "frozen" provenance record can be silently altered after construction.
11	
12	:class:`FrozenDict` fixes both: it is an immutable :class:`~collections.abc.Mapping` and is
13	hashable. :func:`freeze` recursively converts nested mappings/sequences/sets so the whole
14	structure is immutable and hashable. Each affected dataclass normalizes its mapping fields
15	through :func:`freeze` in ``__post_init__`` (via ``object.__setattr__`` to satisfy frozen).
16	"""
17	
18	from __future__ import annotations
19	
20	from collections.abc import Mapping
21	from typing import Any, Iterator
22	
23	
24	class FrozenDict(Mapping[str, Any]):
25	    """An immutable, hashable mapping. Item assignment is impossible; hashing is stable."""
26	
27	    __slots__ = ("_data", "_hash")
28	
29	    def __init__(self, data: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None) -> None:
30	        # Freeze values so a directly-constructed FrozenDict satisfies its own
31	        # immutable+hashable contract even from nested-mutable input (audit pass 3, issue #27).
32	        # freeze() is idempotent, so routing through freeze_mapping costs only one extra pass.
33	        self._data: dict[str, Any] = (
34	            {k: freeze(v) for k, v in data.items()} if data is not None else {}
35	        )
36	        self._hash: int | None = None
37	
38	    def __getitem__(self, key: str) -> Any:
39	        return self._data[key]
40	
41	    def __iter__(self) -> Iterator[str]:
42	        return iter(self._data)
43	
44	    def __len__(self) -> int:
45	        return len(self._data)
46	
47	    def __repr__(self) -> str:
48	        return f"FrozenDict({self._data!r})"
49	
50	    def __hash__(self) -> int:
51	        if self._hash is None:
52	            self._hash = hash(frozenset(self._data.items()))
53	        return self._hash
54	
55	    def __eq__(self, other: object) -> bool:
56	        if isinstance(other, Mapping):
57	            return dict(self._data) == dict(other)
58	        return NotImplemented
59	
60	
61	def freeze(value: Any) -> Any:
62	    """Recursively convert ``value`` into an immutable, hashable form.
63	
64	    Mappings → :class:`FrozenDict`, lists/tuples → tuples, sets → frozensets. Known mutable
65	    buffers are converted to hashable equivalents (``bytearray`` → ``bytes``;
66	    ``array.array`` / numpy-like arrays exposing ``tobytes`` → a ``(kind, shape, bytes)``
67	    tuple). Any remaining leaf that is not hashable raises ``TypeError`` *here, at
68	    construction time* — rather than silently passing through and resurfacing the error the
69	    moment the record is hashed (audit pass 2, issue #19; the incomplete half of #7).
70	    """
71	    if isinstance(value, FrozenDict):
72	        return value
73	    if isinstance(value, Mapping):
74	        return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
75	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
76	        return tuple(freeze(v) for v in value)
77	    if isinstance(value, (set, frozenset)):
78	        return frozenset(freeze(v) for v in value)
79	    if isinstance(value, bytearray):
80	        return bytes(value)
81	    # Duck-typed buffer/array support (e.g. array.array, numpy.ndarray) without importing
82	    # numpy: an array-like exposing tobytes() is canonicalized to a hashable descriptor tuple.
83	    # Require an array-ish marker (shape/typecode/dtype) so an unrelated object that merely
84	    # happens to have a tobytes() is left to the hashability check below (fix-review nit, #19).
85	    tobytes = getattr(value, "tobytes", None)
86	    array_like = hasattr(value, "shape") or hasattr(value, "typecode") or hasattr(value, "dtype")
87	    if callable(tobytes) and array_like and not isinstance(value, (bytes, str)):
88	        shape = getattr(value, "shape", None)
89	        kind = getattr(getattr(value, "dtype", None), "str", None) or getattr(
90	            value, "typecode", type(value).__name__
91	        )
92	        # Route the descriptor through freeze() so a malformed tobytes() returning a
93	        # non-hashable value is still caught loud at construction (audit pass 3, issue #26).
94	        return freeze((str(kind), tuple(shape) if shape is not None else None, tobytes()))
95	    try:
96	        hash(value)
97	    except TypeError as exc:
98	        raise TypeError(
99	            f"freeze: leaf of type {type(value).__name__!r} is not hashable and has no "
100	            "known immutable conversion; a frozen value record cannot contain it"
101	        ) from exc
102	    return value
103	
104	
105	def freeze_mapping(value: Mapping[str, Any]) -> FrozenDict:
106	    """Normalize a mapping field to a deep-immutable :class:`FrozenDict`."""
107	    return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
108

cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
from assay_engine.pipeline import GateReview
from assay_engine.orchestration.phases import Phase
from assay_engine._frozen import FrozenDict
phases = list(Phase)
p1, p2 = phases[0], phases[1]
d = {'a': {'b': [1,2]}, 'c': 1}
gr = GateReview(gate='g', frm=p1, to=p2, summary='s', payload=d)
print('payload type:', type(gr.payload).__name__)
print('is FrozenDict:', isinstance(gr.payload, FrozenDict))
print('nested frozen:', isinstance(gr.payload['a'], FrozenDict))
# mutation attempt on original dict does not affect frozen copy
d['a']['b'].append(3)
print('isolation - frozen still 2 elems:', gr.payload['a']['b'])
# hashable
print('hash ok:', hash(gr) is not None)
# in-place mutation impossible
try:
    gr.payload['x'] = 1
    print('MUTABLE - BUG')
except TypeError as e:
    print('immutable OK:', type(e).__name__)
"

content.text:
The fix works correctly at runtime. The PoC confirms all three aspects of the original finding are resolved: payload is deep-frozen (FrozenDict, nested too), the record is hashable, and the payload is immutable in place. The fix mirrors the exact pattern used by every sibling frozen record (Verdict, etc.) and is the precise change suggested by the finding.

Now assessing completeness. The fix is correct and addresses the root cause soundly. However, there is NO regression test that constructs a `GateReview` and asserts its payload is frozen/immutable/hashable. The diff to tests does not add any such test, and the existing test_immutability.py (which tests sibling records like Verdict, Unit, BaselineArtifact) has no GateReview case. A regression (e.g., someone removing the `__post_init__`) would not be caught by any test — the existing pipeline tests at lines 79/223 only read `payload["..."]`, which works fine on a plain dict too, so they would not detect the regression.

input.reasoning: The applied fix adds a __post_init__ to GateReview that routes payload through freeze_mapping via object.__setattr__, exactly the sibling-record pattern used by ProvenanceEntry/Verdict/etc. and exactly the suggested_fix. It is sound and addresses the root cause (no hack/over-claim). PoC against the venv confirms all three failure modes from the finding are resolved: (1) payload becomes a deep FrozenDict (nested mappings/lists frozen too), so it is no longer mutable in place (gr.payload['x']=1 raises TypeError) and is isolated from later mutation of the source dict; (2) hash(gr) succeeds (previously the auto-generated __hash__ over a dict field would raise TypeError on hash); (3) immutability holds. So 'appropriate' is true. However 'complete' is false: there is NO regression test for GateReview. The test diff (git diff main...HEAD -- tests/) adds nothing referencing GateReview; tests/test_immutability.py tests siblings (Verdict, Unit, BaselineArtifact, FrozenDict) but never GateReview; and the only pipeline tests touching a GateReview payload merely do payload['decision']/payload['error_type'] reads, which succeed equally on a plain dict — so removing the __post_init__ would NOT fail any test. A regression that reverts the fix would go undetected.
input.evidence: Fix at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py lines 90-92 (__post_init__ calling freeze_mapping, import added line 38). freeze_mapping defined in src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:105-107. PoC output: payload type=FrozenDict; nested frozen=True; isolation frozen still (1,2) after source .append(3); hash ok=True; in-place set raised TypeError. Missing test: `grep -rn GateReview tests/` returns nothing; `git diff main...HEAD -- tests/ | grep GateReview` returns nothing; tests/test_immutability.py has no GateReview case; tests/test_pipeline.py lines 79/223 only read payload[...] which also works on a plain dict.

content.text:
Verification complete for finding #113.

Summary: The fix is **appropriate but incomplete**.

- **Appropriate (true):** The `__post_init__` added to `GateReview` in `/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py` (lines 90-92) routes `payload` through `freeze_mapping` via `object.__setattr__`, matching the exact sibling-record pattern and the suggested fix. PoC against the venv confirmed all three failure modes are resolved: payload is now a deep `FrozenDict`, hashing succeeds, in-place mutation raises `TypeError`, and the frozen copy is isolated from later mutation of the source dict.
- **Complete (false):** No regression test exists for `GateReview`. The test diff adds nothing referencing it, `test_immutability.py` covers siblings but not `GateReview`, and the only pipeline tests touching a review payload merely read keys (which works on a plain dict too). Reverting the `__post_init__` would not fail any test.
